sentences.text: My Uncle Baruch, my mother's favorite brother, my favorite uncle, he had a coat, a great-- great tweed coat that had a-- a fur inside.
And this-- the fur we had to take out and give it to the Nazis because they took everything that had fur.
So my mother took out the fur from inside that-- didn't give up the whole coat-- and gave the fur away.
And the coat was hanging there.
When I adopted my brothers, I lost the legal right as a child.
They were getting some clothes from social services.
So because we had no-- things were falling apart-- and I didn't have much.
And they felt so bad that, you know, Mommy would be unhappy to look-- the schmattas that you're wearing, that you're not dressed properly.
So what they did, they [SIGHS] took the bread to a tailor, a uncle of a friend of mine, and took the coat that was hanging by us.
And they told him to take the inside of the coat, where the fabric still looked fresh, and make a suit for me.
And that was the suit that I came to Auschwitz, wearing at Auschwitz, going to suit.
You were still in the ghetto when they made the suit?
Yes.
Yes, I was still in the ghetto.
My mother was very close to that uncle.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was-- was a rich family life between her sisters, one sister, and this brother.
His name was [YIDDISH], and Uncle Baruch, her brother.
And then she had two brothers that died, I think '36.
I remember, I wrote down the dates, before the war.
They both had cancer.
But she was close with her sister-in-laws and with the children.
And one of those sister-in-laws had moved in with us, in-- into the ghetto.
Well, we were locked in May 1st, 1940.
My mother was taken away September 10, 1942.
So it's about-- more than two years.
When my mother was home, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had worked in a different tailor shop, together with my mother.
And I was doing the hand work on the uniforms, where she was doing more major work.
I mean, she-- she knew what she was doing.

And I would just-- knew how to do-- use a needle, and I was doing the finishing or taking out with scissors the little threads, or cleaning up the-- the [? forms. ?] And when my mother was taken away, and I was sick, loss of calcium, and I couldn't move.
And everybody at that point had to work.
Because everybody, no matter what age, was taught to do something.
But I couldn't move.
I couldn't go to the factory.
So what I had-- my brothers-- there was a factory that was making rugs.
In order to make those rounded-- those-- you know, the-- those oval rugs.
Those--
Rag.
What are they called?
Rag rugs.
Yeah, rag rugs, that-- that you braid them.
There I got an assignment to do the braiding.
But I couldn't go to the factory.
So they would go to the factory, bring rolls of long strips of fabric.
And I would braid it laying in a bed.
And they would bring it back to the factories.
So I wouldn't get a soup, but I was legally employed.
Because otherwise you-- you would be deported.
And then after-- eventually, when I-- I got better, I went to the factory.
[SIGHS] What did we eat?
The ration of bread, which didn't last too long.
Sometimes, you would get a little extra potatoes.
So we would try to make delicacies out of it, or some extra vegetables.
I don't remember getting milk too often.
We would get saccharin, which we didn't get any sugar.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Very poorly-- very, very little and very poorly.
My aunt [SIGHS] and her children moved out.
I think it was about '41.
Because the ghetto was getting roomier.
People were dying out.
So they got a-- an apartment.
And that other couple too moved out.
So at that time it was just my mother, my three brothers, and I till 1942.
So when my mother was taken away, it was just us in-- in that apartment.
They too were sent to tailor shops.
And they were taught how to do handiwork, whatever.
They didn't use machines.
I think they were too little for the machines.
But they get them some-- whatever work they could do, clean up or nothing major.
But as long as they're in a shop, and as long as they listed as employed people, we thought they were safe.
Yes.
When it was getting cold, we wouldn't get enough coal, and we were freezing.
And that's why everything that was available for firework-- for firewood would be used.
The-- the apartment that we lived had a wall made out of wood.
It reached almost to ceiling, like a long divider.
That was there all the years we lived there in the ghetto.
We broke it down, bit by bit, not all at one time, little by little.
Then we started burning furniture.
Stealing wood, which was punishable by deportation, until we had to move out of the house that we lived on [POLISH] 16.
Because it was a wooden structures, and they started taking the wooden structures apart to-- to make-- to use for wood, to give it out as rations for wood.
Very little coal would come in.
And then we moved to a brick building, which we stayed until we deported.
But the cold weather was-- was a disaster.
People were getting sick from the cold and-- and freezing to death.
There were all different emotions.
[SIGHS] We were angry, but in the same time we felt-- at least the group that I belonged to-- that we have to continue education.
We have to study.
We have to read.
We have to be prepared for tomorrow, because living one more day, that was our way of fighting back.
They didn't kill me yet.
I'm still here.
And that particular group, we'd kind of encourage each other, have little secret questions and study history and-- and languages and sciences, and write jokes, tell jokes, songs, and put on plays.
And in our house we had a secret library.
We had 300 books.
We're ?] supposed to have any books.
And that wasn't only defiance.
I think it was spiritual resistance.
We had-- my brothers made shelves.
And I made curtains.
There was some fabric laying in our house.
It belonged once to a friend of my mother's.
She had a store where they would sell-- selling fabrics.
And she put that in our house.
There was no one there to--
So I took some of that fabric, and I made curtains.
And it looked like a drapery would be covering a window.
And we had books there.
And people would come in, no library cards, nothing, no system.
They would just take the books, put it in their clothing, and if they lived, they returned it.
So the group that I belonged to, we were kind of each other's strength.
And I think this too helped.
Were these boys and girls?
Yeah.
Oh, [? were the ?] mostly teenagers.
But we were each other's moral support.
And--
Oh, yeah.
There was Yulek.
He was my friend, his sister Faygele.
There was my friend Karola, who survived.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [?
Henek. ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Laibish, Rifkele.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was a large group.
At-- at the moment these just-- those stick out, you know.
Yeah.
Yeah, I-- I always liked to read.
And in fact, I made use of that.
Because even as a child, when I read and I kind of remembered what I read and I would retell it, sometimes I was working in the street for entertainment.
So I would kind of tell what I just read.
And sometimes kids would take advantage, like they had a homework assignment to write a book report, and they didn't read the book.
So they asked me about the book, and I would tell them, and they write a book report.
[LAUGHS]
So in the ghetto, I would read books.
In fact, when we were standing, when they were cutting the fabrics, and those people, they-- to me they were older.
Because they [? already ?] had children.
There was a woman whose children were taken away.
And I-- I was the kid over there.
And I would tell, and sometimes now I think like it would be soap opera.
I would tell them, you know, the chapter of that book.
Or would tell them Sholem Aleichem stories.
And-- and-- and they would love it.
Even to my brother Laibele, when he was sick I would tell him Sholem Aleichem stories.
I would read or tell by heart what I-- what I remembered.
So I made use of my ability to read and to like to read and to remember.
Not everybody had this strength or the-- the inclination to read.
But it was amazing, is in the creativity that-- that came out of the ghettos, especially Lodz Ghetto.
It was a big, big, big place.
He got tuberculosis.
Right, yeah, begin.
Right in the beginning he got tuberculosis.
And then there was no medicine, there were no doctors.
And they kept on saying he needs food.
He needs fresh air.
He needs medicine, and we didn't have any of those.
By reading to him, by telling him stories, by telling him jokes.
And he had a wonderful sense of humor.
By entertaining each other, and a lot of times he stayed by himself.
[SIGHS] We-- we had a root cellar, when-- you know, when like Mr.
Brown ?] would deliver the potatoes to us, where we kept it for the winter, vegetables and potatoes, and coal before the war.
So in that root cellar, we put blankets in it and cushions and put him there.
And covered it with a trapdoor, put a table-- a rug and a table over it.
So it didn't show that he was hidden.
When the guards came in, they didn't-- they didn't find him.
They didn't find him.
Oh, just a few hours.
Well, in the ghetto by itself, we didn't have to hide him.
Because the-- the Nazis would not come into the ghetto.
And the Jewish police only come-- came to look if there's-- of those people who were on a list for deportation.
He was never on a list for deportation.
So they didn't come to look for him.
So we really didn't have to hide him.
We only hid him that time when we went out, when they surrounded the ghetto, when my mother was taken away.
So just the time that he was really hidden, in that-- in that root cellar.
Otherwise, we-- we didn't hide him.
1943, April 23rd.
He just died, couldn't go on any longer.
Mm-hmm.
After.
[DRINKS]
[SIGHS] When she stood outside, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SOBBING] she showed her papers.
[PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] And she said, I am a good worker.
And the-- [INAUDIBLE] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SOBBING] the guard said, no, you are a sick old woman.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they-- they pulled her-- [SOBS] I'm sorry.
[DRINKS] They pulled her on a wagon, and the Jewish police held her back.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they sped off.
[SOBBING] Right next to her.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] I was at the line, you know, for the selections.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And Motele and Moishele and I, we were sent back to the ones that remained in the ghetto.
The ones for deportation were put in the wagon.
[SIGHS] The [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] welfare department of the ghetto had found out that we're four children without supervision.
So they-- there were a lot of people that their children were taken away.
So they felt that they could divide us, and the people that lost their children would take us in.
[SIGHS] But they wanted to take Laibele and put him in an orphanage.
And I knew that an orphanage-- he would be deported.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So the-- we refused to let them do that.
And they-- they weren't too happy about it.
The social worker would be coming to the house and trying to convince me that this is the best and, you're only children.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At one point [SIGHS] the head of social services, the [NON-ENGLISH], had called me in.
And she-- she called in Motele, she called in Moishele.
And she said she's going to adopt me.
Now, this would be like temptation, because she lived on much better conditions.
I didn't think she was hungry, because they-- they had-- they still had the luxury life.
But I should let them get-- Motele and Moishele to other people, the people that their children were taken.
And Laibele will go to the orphanage.
And I got angry.
And I said, you're not gonna do it.
We don't-- we don't want anything.
I guess I touched them or something.
Because they made a new law.
They made me a legal guardian, and I was 16.
And we stayed together.
We stayed-- they even came, gave the children clothing.
Because now they're adopted children, so there were certain rules and regulations, some special food.
For me, I lost the child-- what I am-- the rights as a child.
But I remember they gave me shoes to make up for whatever because I-- I needed shoes.
So she kind of bent the rule and-- and gave me shoes.
And then they-- they came, they bought me [NON-ENGLISH].
Because my legs-- [NON-ENGLISH] was vitamins, liquid vitamins at whatever place they got it.
And we stayed together until Laibele died.
And then we stayed together, Motele and Moishele, until deportation.
The deportation was in August 1944.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They closed the factories.
After that time, everybody had to work.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When they closed the-- before they closed the factories there was Hans Biebow, the SS Commandant for Lodz ghetto.
And he came into the factories, and he [SIGHS] stood there, soft-spoken with a smile on his face.
I look at his pictures.
It doesn't say that he's a murderer.
And he said, Meine Juden.
My Jews, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
My ladies and gentlemen, that's how he spoke to us.
And he was telling us that the Russians army's near Warsaw.
And we need your labor.
And therefore we are going to move you to different places of work, and not a hair is going to be touched on your head.
And he offered an extra loaf of bread.
And he closed the factories.
We want families to stay together.
We don't want anybody to get lost.
And he closed the factories.
So when he closed the factories, we weren't useful anymore.
We weren't productive anymore.
And the major meal was gone.
And people started taking their precious belongings and go to the trains.
They didn't call them cattle cars.
Go to the trains.
My brothers and I-- it was just Motele, Moishele, and I.
And we had two friends, Laibish-- [SIGHS] no, there were four friends.
Laibish, Karola, Berl-- Karola's brother-- and Laibish's sister, Rifkele.
We try hiding.
And we were running out of food.
We were hoping that if we could hold out, the Russians will come and liberate us.
But we were running out of food, and the ghetto was gettin' smaller and smaller.
It was gettin' easier to be caught.
So we too went to the trains.
And that was in August 1944.
And I wore that suit, that Karola's uncle had made, that the-- my kids, my brothers paid with the bread.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I took my mother's picture, this one.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I took a-- [SOBBING] a journal [DRINKS] I wrote in the ghetto.
I wrote a-- a journal, all letters addressed to my sisters, my brother, my mother, believing that they survive, and [SIGHS] telling them what we did, what we wouldn't do in the ghetto.
We even joked, when they come back, we'll give them their mail.
That was their mail.
And [SIGHS] if they do not come back, it will bear witness.
And this I took with me to Auschwitz.
Well, we had bundles.
We were allowed to take whatever was of value.
We didn't have to hide it.
Because he wanted us to take everything that was of value and stay together.
So that-- to me this was of value.
So that's what I took.
Oh, yes.
I don't think anybody ever forgets it.
We were [SIGHS] put 80 people in a cattle car.
We were locked in.
And there was a bucket as the toilet.
And we were squashed together, literally squashed together.
And it took three days.
Meanwhile, people would try to encourage, oh, they need our labor.
Nothing is going to happen to us.
We're productive.
They have all that free labor.
They're all big philosophers, tried to figure out why.
Why, why, why would they hurt us?
They need our labor.
And some people would be praying, and some would be crying, and one woman went crazy.
But it took three days.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And when we came to Auschwitz, we didn't know it existed.
The Polish name was Oswiecim.
When they opened the cattle cars, there were a lot of dead people.
And there were guards [SIGHS] with nightsticks.
We-- first time when we saw people in uniforms, in striped uniforms, we didn't know what that meant.
And we were greeted by a band that was playing.
There was a wonderful talented group of musicians that they kept.
They were literally playing for their lives.
They were playing for the guards, and then they were-- they were giving concerts, classical music, no less, for the-- for the-- the officers to entertain them after the hard work of killing.
And they greeted the transports to add to the confusion.
And we saw the sign Arbeit macht frei.
And we didn't know at that time that the gentleman saying [GERMAN], left-right, left-right, is Dr.
Mengele.
But he was always there greeting, looking for people for experiments.
We found out once we were separated.
Because at that moment, we didn't know it.
But we were separated, men and women separate.
That's the last time I saw my brothers because they were-- went with the men.
And we had to drop all our clothes.
And our hair was shaved.
It-- we-- the people at the left-- again, we didn't know where they were going.
They were marched supposedly to showers.
But-- and they told them to put the clothes neatly.
And they went into the showers.
They were gas chambers.
There was no shower.
The ones to the right were sent to the camp where they told us to drop our clothes.
And afterwards they shaved our heads.
And we went into like a bluish water, like a low-- like a basin.
I don't know.
It looked like a swimming pool.
A very low pool of water with-- was disinfectant, that we-- we're supposed to be sterilized after they shaved the hair.
We went into showers.
There was water-- quick.
But there-- but there was water.
And that's when we find out from the people that were there, because we came into Birkenau.
And Birkenau is the part of Auschwitz where either you went to the gas chambers.
Or if you're lucky, you were sold eventually to slavery.
And the people over there were telling us who that person was that is there because people were there longer than you, and all those things.
I argued with a woman at that time.
She was telling me-- [SIGHS] I smelled the stench, and I saw the smoke.
And she was telling me, they're burning your families here.
And I said, she's a liar.
[SIGHS]
Yeah.
I was with Karola and with Rifkele.
That's the one we went together.
And then when we were put in-- in a barrack which had hundreds of people, and there were five people to a wooden plank.
One girl started screaming, she knew Rifkele.
And her name is Tola.
And we stayed together.
Yeah.
We managed to stay together.
From Auschwitz we were transported together, and we stayed till liberation together.
And Tola and Karola and I still stayed together.
Rifkele's in Israel.
We were marched naked [SIGHS] after the hair was shaved.
And when we came in after the shower, a quick shower, and we were naked.
And they gave whatever-- there was a pile of clothing, and they gave whatever.
And they gave me a long petticoat.
That was my clothing.
Rifkele was tall.
I was short.
And they gave Rifkele like a baby-doll dress.
So-- and-- and she was embarrassed, you know?
We were still modest.
So we switched.
I gave her the long petticoat, and this covered-- that little baby-doll dress was good enough for me.
Because it covered me.
But for the appells outside, we had to go naked.
So they could see-- we couldn't hide anything.
So that we still-- the bones there.
And whoever couldn't stay straight and fell down, that was dead.
It was August.
[SIGHS] We came in-- we left the ghetto August 24.
We came in August 27.
And it was one week.
But it seemed like I was there forever.

Because I-- I guess I was the lucky one because I was sold-- literally sold as a slave, to go to the-- a factory in Germany, to work in a factory that was making parts, [BACKROUND NOISE] parts to airplanes.
But--
[SIGHS] They put us on trucks.
And it so happened that Karola and Tola, Rifkele, we all were on the same truck.
They didn't tattoo us in Auschwitz.
But when we came to the camp, they gave us number, where we were standing.
There was no specific system.
And mine was 55082.
We came-- they-- they gave whatever to, what they had piles of clothing.
We were 400 girls there.
It was a camp called Mittelsteine in Germany.
We were sold to a factory that was making parts to airplanes.
And we all had the same shoes, though.
The clothes is whatever they had.
But they had the same shoes, which is wooden bottoms and rough canvas tops.
And it was literally pulling the skin of our feet, as we were always being chased.
We were put in barracks, 50 girls in a barrack.
